parts.title: Transcript
parts.annotated_transcript_html:
Speaker 1: You now have some time to look at the questions on your screen. Now answer the questions on your screen. So now that we're sure about the topic, let's get some ideas down into the project plan.

Speaker 2: Firstly, what research are we building from? Well, several of the studies I looked at showed quite clearly that stimuli to the olfactory system can influence how humans behave. That sounds like quite a solid basis for us to work from.

Speaker 3: Yeah, and we can make reference to anecdotal evidence too, such as the effect the smell of roasted coffee has on house viewers, or so they say.

Speaker 1: Okay, great. So the aim of the study is to determine if the addition of certain fragrances to an environment affects human judgment. So how are we going to test that?

Speaker 2: Well, we have to create a situation in which the participants need to make simple decisions in the presence and absence of fragrance. A simple two-group experiment seems appropriate.

Speaker 3: Okay, so what fragrances shall we choose? And what decision? These are the key variables.

Speaker 1: You're right. Let's start with the fragrances. How many do we need? Should we have one positive, one neutral, and one negative?

Speaker 2: I think we want to start simple and build on it if we notice a pattern. So how about one pleasant-smelling aroma?

Speaker 3: Yes, okay. We can do a Google search to find one of the most popular ones. What about the decision the participants are to come to?

Speaker 1: Hmm. Well, we want to gather quantitative yes, no, or numerical data from 1 to 10, which we can analyze easily.

Speaker 2: Well, with the two-group method, we could have the groups in separate rooms, one which acts as a control and has a completely neutral odor, the other into which we introduce the fragrance.

Speaker 3: Yes, however, we must be careful to ensure the potency of the odor is kept consistent in this room for all participants. Otherwise, our data won't be valid.

Speaker 1: OK, good point. Now, about the method. Apart from the presence or absence of aroma, the method should be exactly the same. The participant enters, sits down, and what next?

Speaker 2: Well, perhaps we can give a simple informal two-minute presentation about a new product to them.

Speaker 3: Sure, and we can ask them to complete a form with a series of questions to determine their response to the product. For example, on a scale of 1 to 5, how likely would you be to buy this product? Something like that. We can discuss details later anyway.

Speaker 1: OK, what about sample size? Two groups of 12 should be enough to confirm significance, considering that this is really just a pilot.

Speaker 2: What are our selection criteria?

Speaker 3: Well, we just want a broad range of backgrounds and ages, really. I'll advertise the experiment on the department portal.

Speaker 1: Oh, and we'll have to be careful not to get any participants with allergies, for obvious reasons.

Speaker 2: OK, good point. Right, should we go and take this to Professor Ingram for approval? We can ask him about possible rooms to use too.

Speaker 3: All right, let's go!
